title: Server-Sent Events Deep Dive: The Event Stream Format
description: The precise SSE wire format from the WHATWG HTML spec: comment/keep-alive lines, multi-line data, the dispatch rule, named vs default events, the `id` and `retry` fields, stopping reconnection, and the reverse-proxy buffering gotcha. The parsing behavior here is verified with a spec-faithful parser in Node.

questions.title: In an SSE stream, what does a line beginning with a colon (`:`) do?
questions.description: For example, a server periodically writing `: keep-alive` followed by a blank line.

options.text: It's a comment — ignored by the client; servers use it as a keep-alive heartbeat to hold a quiet connection open.
It sets the event's id.
It immediately terminates the stream.
It defines a custom event type.

questions.explanation:
A line that starts with `:` is a **comment** and is discarded by the parser. Its main practical use is as a heartbeat:

```text
: keep-alive

data: actual payload

```

Sending a comment every 15–30s keeps idle-timeout intermediaries (proxies, load balancers) from reaping a connection that has no data flowing, and helps surface a dead connection. It carries no payload and never reaches your `onmessage` handler.

questions.title: A single SSE event contains two `data:` lines. How is the payload delivered to the client?
questions.description: The event has `data: line1` then `data: line2`, then a blank line.

options.text: The two values are concatenated with a newline (`\n`) between them into one `event.data` string.
Two separate `message` events fire, one per line.
Only the last `data:` line is kept; earlier ones are discarded.
The values are concatenated with no separator at all.

questions.explanation:
Each `data:` line appends its value **plus a line feed** to the event's data buffer; when the event dispatches, the single trailing line feed is removed. The net effect is that multiple `data:` lines are joined by `\n`. Verified with a spec-faithful parser:

```text
data: line1
data: line2

```

produces `event.data === "line1\nline2"`. This is how a server sends a multi-line string (e.g. a pretty-printed JSON block) inside one logical event — you split it across `data:` lines and the client rejoins them.

questions.title: What part of the SSE wire format signals "this event is complete — dispatch it now"?
questions.description: Two words. It's what separates one event's fields from the next event's.
questions.explanation:
An event is dispatched when the parser reads a **blank line** (an empty line, i.e. two consecutive newlines — the previous line's terminator plus an empty line). Until that blank line, `event:`, `data:`, `id:`, and `retry:` lines accumulate into the current event; the blank line flushes them.

One consequence: forgetting the trailing blank line is the most common SSE bug — the browser holds the event, waiting for a terminator that never comes, so nothing fires. Every event must end with `\n\n`.

questions.title: Your server sends events tagged `event: price`, but the client's `onmessage` handler never fires. Why?
questions.description: The connection is open and bytes are arriving — yet `onmessage` stays silent.

options.text: `onmessage` only receives events of the default type `message`; a named event needs `addEventListener("price", …)`.
Named events aren't supported by `EventSource` at all.
Named events require `Content-Type: text/named-event-stream`.
Named events only start firing after the first `id:` is received.

questions.title: Which statements about SSE field parsing are correct?
questions.description: Select every true statement about how each line is interpreted.

options.text: A single leading space after the field's colon is stripped, so `data: hi` and `data:hi` both yield `hi`.
Field names other than `event`, `data`, `id`, and `retry` are ignored.
A line with no colon is treated as a field name whose value is the empty string.
A field value can span multiple lines by ending a line with a backslash for continuation.

questions.explanation:
The parser splits each line at the first colon: the part before is the field, the part after is the value, and **exactly one** leading space of the value is removed (so `data: hi` → `hi`, and a value that genuinely needs a leading space must be written `data:  hi`). Only four field names are meaningful — anything else is silently ignored. A line with no colon (e.g. a bare `data`) is a field with an empty value.

There is **no** backslash line-continuation; to send multiple lines you repeat the `data:` field, and the client joins them with `\n` (see the multi-line question).

questions.title: What does a `retry: 10000` line do, and what's the constraint on its value?

options.text: It sets the client's reconnection delay to 10000 milliseconds; the value must be ASCII digits only, or the line is ignored.
It limits the stream to 10000 events before the server closes it.
It sets a 10000-second server-side idle timeout.
It re-sends the last failed event up to 10000 times.

questions.explanation: `retry:` lets the **server** tune the client's auto-reconnect delay. `retry: 10000` means "if we get disconnected, wait 10000 ms before reconnecting." The value must be ASCII digits; a non-numeric value like `retry: soon` is ignored, leaving the previous (implementation-default, typically a few seconds) delay in place. It applies to future reconnections, not to the current event — which is why a `retry:` line is often sent on its own followed by a blank line.

questions.title: An `EventSource` reconnects on its own after a drop. Which 2xx HTTP status should the server return on (re)connect to make it give up and stop reconnecting?
questions.description: The idiomatic "we're done, don't come back" response. Give the numeric status code.
questions.explanation:
EventSource only auto-reconnects after a *transient* failure (a dropped connection). When it (re)connects and the response isn't `200 OK` with `Content-Type: text/event-stream`, the client treats it as a **permanent failure**: it sets `readyState` to `CLOSED`, fires `error`, and does **not** try again. Returning **204 No Content** is the conventional, idiomatic 2xx way to tell a client "stop reconnecting" — the server cleanly signals there's nothing more to stream.

So the lifecycle is: network blip → auto-retry (with `Last-Event-ID`); deliberate `204` → permanent stop.

questions.title: SSE works locally, but in production behind nginx the events arrive in big delayed bursts instead of one-by-one. Most likely cause and fix?
questions.description: The stream is correct on the server; something between server and browser is holding it.

options.text: The reverse proxy is buffering the response; disable it (nginx `proxy_buffering off;` or send the `X-Accel-Buffering: no` header) and flush after each event.
SSE isn't allowed over HTTPS; switch the endpoint to `ws://`.
The browser is caching events; append a random query parameter to bust it.
`text/event-stream` must be gzip-compressed; enable compression to fix delivery.

questions.explanation: By default many reverse proxies (and some app servers) **buffer** an upstream response, releasing it only once a chunk fills or the response ends — which batches your individual events into delayed bursts and defeats the whole point of SSE. The fix is to turn buffering off for the stream: nginx `proxy_buffering off;` or have the app send the `X-Accel-Buffering: no` response header, and make sure your handler flushes after writing each event. Response **compression** makes it worse (another buffer), so disable it for the stream too. HTTPS and browser caching are red herrings here.

questions.title: An event sets `id: 42`. The next event has no `id:` line. If the client reconnects after that second event, what `Last-Event-ID` does it send?
questions.description: Think about whether the last event ID is per-event or sticky.

options.text: `42` — the last event ID persists across events until a new `id:` field changes it.
Empty — an event without `id:` resets the last event ID.
`43` — the client auto-increments the id for you.
It depends on the most recent `retry:` value.

questions.explanation:
The "last event ID" is **sticky**: an `id:` field updates it, and it then carries forward to every subsequent event until another `id:` changes it (an `id:` with an empty value resets it to the empty string). Verified with a parser — the third event below, which has no `id:`, still reports `lastEventId` `42`:

```text
id: 42
data: a

data: b

```

On reconnect the client sends `Last-Event-ID: 42`. This is what lets a server tag only some events (say, checkpoints) yet still resume correctly.

questions.title: Which statements about `EventSource` reconnection are correct?

options.text: On a dropped connection (network error), it fires `error`, enters CONNECTING, and retries automatically.
On reconnect it sends a `Last-Event-ID` header with the last id it received.
A non-200 response (such as 204) on connect is a permanent failure — it goes to CLOSED and does not retry.
Calling `close()` merely pauses the stream; it auto-resumes after the retry interval.

questions.explanation: EventSource distinguishes *transient* from *permanent* failures. A network drop is transient: it fires `error`, sets `readyState` to `CONNECTING` (0), waits the retry delay, and reconnects — sending `Last-Event-ID` so the server can resume. A bad response (non-200 / wrong content type, e.g. `204`) is permanent: `readyState` becomes `CLOSED` (2) and it never retries. And `close()` is explicit, permanent teardown — there is no auto-resume; you must construct a new `EventSource` to start again.

questions.title: Why do SSE servers periodically send a comment line (e.g. `: ping`) even when there's no real data to deliver?
questions.description: Think about a connection that stays quiet for minutes at a time.

options.text: To stop idle-timeout intermediaries (proxies, load balancers) from closing a quiet connection — and to help detect a dead one.
Because `EventSource` errors out if it receives no bytes within one second.
Because each comment increments the Last-Event-ID counter.
Because the spec requires a comment between every pair of events.

questions.explanation: A long-lived SSE connection that goes quiet is at the mercy of every box between server and browser: proxies, load balancers, and NAT devices often close connections idle for some seconds/minutes. Periodically writing a comment line (which the client ignores) keeps bytes flowing so the connection isn't reaped, and a failed write surfaces a broken pipe so the server can clean up. It is purely operational — not required by the protocol between events, and unrelated to ids or any 1-second rule.
